content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Megan K. Garibaldi 
Assistant City Attorney 
City of Villa Park 
Rutan & Tucker, LLP 
611 Anton Blvd., Suite 1400 
Costa Mesa, CA 92626 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-15-083 
Dear Ms. Garibaldi: 
May 27, 2015 
This letter responds to your request for advice, on behalf of Villa Park Councilmember 
Robert Collacott, regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the 
"Act").1 This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission does 
not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) 
Additionally, the Commission does not provide advice relating to past conduct. This letter should 
not be construed as assistance on any conduct that may have already taken place. (See 
Regulation l 8329(b)(8)(A).) 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict 
of interest or Section 1090. 
QUESTION 
Under the newly adopted public generally regulation, may Councilmember Collacott talce 
part in a governmental decision to approve or amend residential parking requirements proposed 
on the street on which he resides, as well as several nearby streets? 
CONCLUSION 
Based upon the facts provided, Councilmember Collacott has established that he may 
take part in a decision regarding the parking restrictions under the public generally exception. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references arc to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS 
File No. A-15-083 
Page No. 2 
Your office serves as the City Attorney for the City of Villa Park ("City"). On behalf of 
Councilmember Collacott, you are requesting advice pursuant to Section 83114(b). 
Councilmember Collacott is currently serving his first term on the Villa Park City Council and 
was elected in November of 2014. He presently owns a single-family home located on 
Featherhill Drive, which is his personal residence. 
In 2014, prior to Councilmember Collacott' s election to the City Council, a local citizens 
group approached the City regarding concerns related to parking, traffic, and safety on 
Featherhill Drive, Mountain View Circle, Brynmar Drive, and Dodson Way (the "Identified 
Streets"). In response to these concerns, the city proposed parking restrictions for the Identified 
Streets in an effort to address the parking, traffic, and safety concerns resulting from two nearby 
schools and after school activities conducted on the schools' campuses. Specifically, "parking by 
permit only" restrictions were proposed. 
Limited "parking by permit only" restrictions have existed for decades on Featherhill 
Drive on weekends and holidays. Proposed parking restrictions expanded the restrictions to the 
Identified Streets and increased the restrictions to seven days a week from 7:00 a.m. to 7:00 p.m. 
Citizen petitions submitted to the City reflecting the homeowners' interest on the Identified 
Streets with respect to the proposed parking restrictions. 
On September 23, 2014, prior to Councilmember Collacott's election, the Villa Park City 
Council took public testimony and considered the submitted petitions, correspondence in 
opposition to the parking restrictions, and other evidence related to the proposed parking 
restrictions. The City Council than voted to authorize temporary "parking by permit only" 
restrictions on Featherhill Drive, Mountain View Circle, and Dodson Way ("Modified Identified 
Streets"), operative seven days a week from 7:00 a.m. to 7:00 p.m., for an approximately three­
month trial period. At the same meeting, the City Council further directed staff to return at the 
January 27, 2015 City Council meeting for review of the temporary parking restrictions. 
The City Council again considered the parking restrictions on the Modified Identified 
Streets at its January 27, 2015, meeting. In advance of the agenda item on this matter, 
Councilmember Collacott recused himself from this decision. Without Councilmember 
Collacott's participation, the City Council then took public testimony, considered further 
evidence relating to the matter, and voted to extend the trial period for another three months. The 
City Council agreed to review the parking restrictions at a later City Council meeting after time 
to study the impact of the proposed parking regulations on the Villa Park Little League, which 
uses one of the nearby schools. 
The City Council has not since taken any action on the parking restrictions for the 
Modified Identified Streets. You note that the City's staff will implement the parking restrictions 
and that the City does not anticipate imposing the restrictions by ordinance. 


ANALYSIS 
File No. A-15-083 
Page No. 3 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or 
using his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a 
financial interest. A conflict of interest may arise only when it is reasonably foreseeable that the 
decision will have a material financial effect on the official or his or her immediate family 
"distinguishable from its effect on the public generally." (Section 87103.) 
Under newly adopted Regulation 18703, the financial effect on a public official's 
economic interest is indistinguishable from the effect on the public generally, if the official can 
establish: 
"Limited Neighborhood Effects. The decision affects residential real 
property limited to a specific location, and the decision establishes, amends, or 
eliminates ordinances that restrict on-street parking, impose traffic controls, deter 
vagrancy, reduce nuisance or improve public safety, provided the body making 
the decision gathers sufficient evidence to support the need for the action at the 
specific location." (Regulation l 8703(e)(3).) 
In this case, the decisions regarding the proposed parking restrictions for the Modified 
Identified Streets were initiated by public petitions. Moreover, without Councilmember 
Collacott's participation, the City Council has heard public testimony regarding the restrictions 
and considered evidence related to the restrictions in adopting temporary restrictions. The City 
Council is also awaiting a city study regarding the impact of the restrictions on Villa Park Little 
League. 
Applying Regulation 18703(e)(3), it appears that the decision will affect residential real 
property limited to a specific location and that the City has gathered sufficient evidence to 
support the need for restricting on-street parking. The only remaining question is whether the 
fact that the City does not anticipate adopting the parking restrictions through an ordinance 
precludes the application of the public generally exception for limited neighborhood effects. 
To the extent that the City has determined that it may impose the parking restrictions 
without establishing or amending a city ordinance, we find no compelling reason to require the 
city to establish the restrictions through a formally adopted ordinance to avail itself of the 
exception. Whether the parking restrictions are adopted through a formal ordinance has no 
bearing on the result of the decision. Accordingly, we do not find that the public generally 
exception for limited neighborhood effects requires a formal ordinance where the same result can 
be implemented by a less formal action. In this regard, we find that the use of the word 
"ordinance" in the regulation was not intended to require a formally adopted ordinance but was 
intended to encompass any city rule or ordinance meeting the other requirements of the 
regulation. Based upon the facts provided, Councilmember Collacott has established that he may 
take part in a decision regarding the restrictions under the public generally exception. 


File No. A-15-083 
Page No. 4 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
BGL:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
By: 
Brian G. Lau 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or 
using his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
